IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important, widely recognised problem—modeling word polysemy—by introducing the first Gaussian-mixture probabilistic embedding (w2gm) and an analytic expected-likelihood kernel that preserves scalability. Empirically it shows consistent gains over strong baselines (word2vec sg and unimodal Gaussian w2g) across many standard benchmarks (SimLex, WS variants, MEN, RW, SCWS and entailment datasets), which suggests researchers and practitioners will adopt mixture representations for richer semantics. The analytic tractability and compatibility with skip-gram–style max-margin training make it a practical contribution likely to spawn follow-up work (e.g., energy choices, KL approximations, scalability and component selection), so the impact should be strong though not necessarily transformative across all of NLP. | B(4): Modeling words as Gaussian mixtures directly targets the important, longstanding problem of lexical polysemy with a principled probabilistic formulation; using an analytic expected-likelihood kernel to retain scalability is a practical and useful technical contribution. Demonstrated improvements over point (word2vec) and unimodal Gaussian embeddings on similarity and entailment benchmarks suggest researchers will reconsider probabilistic, multimodal representations for semantics and entailment tasks. The approach opens substantive new directions (sense induction, asymmetric similarity measures, probabilistic downstream models) and is likely to be adopted or extended by others, though it is not yet clearly transformative across all of NLP.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper clearly sits within the core NLP/ML venue scope: it introduces a probabilistic representation (Gaussian mixtures) for word embeddings and evaluates on standard semantic similarity and entailment benchmarks (SimLex, WS, SCWS, Baroni entailment) against strong baselines (word2vec, w2g, and multiple multi-prototype methods). The methodological choices (expected likelihood kernel, max-margin ranking objective) and the empirical protocol (Spearman, AP/F1, KL for asymmetric similarity) align with community expectations for representation learning work. From a reproducibility/relevance standpoint I note missing hyperparameter and code disclosures in the summary, but that does not change that the topic, audience, formulations, and evaluations are a very good fit for a top NLP/ML conference. | B(5): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—word representation learning—with a clear novel formulation (Gaussian mixture embeddings and an analytic expected likelihood kernel) that directly targets polysemy, which is of strong interest to both communities. The work evaluates on a comprehensive set of standard similarity and entailment benchmarks (SimLex, WS variants, SCWS, Baroni et al.) and compares to appropriate baselines (word2vec, unimodal Gaussian embeddings, multi-prototype methods), matching the empirical and evaluative standards expected at top venues. The framing, datasets, and metrics make the paper highly relevant to the conference audience, and the potential impact on sense modeling and probabilistic representations positions it well within the community.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper clearly sits within the core NLP/ML venue scope: it introduces a probabilistic representation (Gaussian mixtures) for word embeddings and evaluates on standard semantic similarity and entailment benchmarks (SimLex, WS, SCWS, Baroni entailment) against strong baselines (word2vec, w2g, and multiple multi-prototype methods). The methodological choices (expected likelihood kernel, max-margin ranking objective) and the empirical protocol (Spearman, AP/F1, KL for asymmetric similarity) align with community expectations for representation learning work. From a reproducibility/relevance standpoint I note missing hyperparameter and code disclosures in the summary, but that does not change that the topic, audience, formulations, and evaluations are a very good fit for a top NLP/ML conference.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—word representation learning—with a clear novel formulation (Gaussian mixture embeddings and an analytic expected likelihood kernel) that directly targets polysemy, which is of strong interest to both communities. The work evaluates on a comprehensive set of standard similarity and entailment benchmarks (SimLex, WS variants, SCWS, Baroni et al.) and compares to appropriate baselines (word2vec, unimodal Gaussian embeddings, multi-prototype methods), matching the empirical and evaluative standards expected at top venues. The framing, datasets, and metrics make the paper highly relevant to the conference audience, and the potential impact on sense modeling and probabilistic representations positions it well within the community.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper extends unimodal Gaussian embeddings (Vilnis & McCallum) to Gaussian mixture word representations and pairs this with an expected likelihood kernel and max-margin training — a technically sensible and non-trivial extension. However, the literature already contains closely related multi-prototype and probabilistic multi-embedding approaches (e.g., “A Probabilistic Model for Learning Multi-Prototype Word Embeddings” and nonparametric multiple-embedding work from 2014), so the claim of being the first probabilistic multi-meaning embedding is overstated. Overall the contribution is a moderate, well-differentiated extension rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, so I rate novelty as incremental-to-moderate. | B(3): The paper advances an intuitive and useful combination—representing words as Gaussian mixtures and using an expected likelihood kernel with a max-margin ranking objective—which offers analytic tractability not present in some earlier approaches. However, the central problem (multi-prototype/multi-sense embeddings) and probabilistic attempts to model multiple meanings are already addressed in closely related prior work (e.g., “A Probabilistic Model for Learning Multi-Prototype Word Embeddings” and nonparametric multi-embedding methods from 2014). Thus the contribution is a non-trivial technical extension (notably the closed-form energy for mixtures and joint learning of mixture parameters) but not a wholly new problem formulation or paradigm, so it represents moderate novelty rather than a breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper extends unimodal Gaussian embeddings (Vilnis & McCallum) to Gaussian mixture word representations and pairs this with an expected likelihood kernel and max-margin training — a technically sensible and non-trivial extension. However, the literature already contains closely related multi-prototype and probabilistic multi-embedding approaches (e.g., “A Probabilistic Model for Learning Multi-Prototype Word Embeddings” and nonparametric multiple-embedding work from 2014), so the claim of being the first probabilistic multi-meaning embedding is overstated. Overall the contribution is a moderate, well-differentiated extension rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, so I rate novelty as incremental-to-moderate.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper advances an intuitive and useful combination—representing words as Gaussian mixtures and using an expected likelihood kernel with a max-margin ranking objective—which offers analytic tractability not present in some earlier approaches. However, the central problem (multi-prototype/multi-sense embeddings) and probabilistic attempts to model multiple meanings are already addressed in closely related prior work (e.g., “A Probabilistic Model for Learning Multi-Prototype Word Embeddings” and nonparametric multi-embedding methods from 2014). Thus the contribution is a non-trivial technical extension (notably the closed-form energy for mixtures and joint learning of mixture parameters) but not a wholly new problem formulation or paradigm, so it represents moderate novelty rather than a breakthrough.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (Gaussian mixture embeddings, use of expected likelihood kernel, max-margin training) are stated early and consistently developed through method and experiments. Notation and writing quality are reported as good, and related work is covered thoroughly, which aids clarity. Presentation could be improved by including reported hyperparameters and code availability (hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned) and by ensuring figures/tables explicitly summarize the many datasets and metrics for easier cross-comparison. | B(4): The paper appears well organized with a clear intro, method, experiments and conclusion, and its main contributions (w2gm mixture model and expected likelihood kernel energy) are stated early. Notation and analytic choices (Gaussian mixtures, expected likelihood kernel) are described clearly and the writing quality is rated good; related work coverage is thorough. However, some presentation details are missing in the summary (no mention of figures/tables effectiveness or reported hyperparameters), which suggests minor gaps in experimental reporting and visualization that prevent an exceptional score.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (Gaussian mixture embeddings, use of expected likelihood kernel, max-margin training) are stated early and consistently developed through method and experiments. Notation and writing quality are reported as good, and related work is covered thoroughly, which aids clarity. Presentation could be improved by including reported hyperparameters and code availability (hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned) and by ensuring figures/tables explicitly summarize the many datasets and metrics for easier cross-comparison.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper appears well organized with a clear intro, method, experiments and conclusion, and its main contributions (w2gm mixture model and expected likelihood kernel energy) are stated early. Notation and analytic choices (Gaussian mixtures, expected likelihood kernel) are described clearly and the writing quality is rated good; related work coverage is thorough. However, some presentation details are missing in the summary (no mention of figures/tables effectiveness or reported hyperparameters), which suggests minor gaps in experimental reporting and visualization that prevent an exceptional score.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper presents a plausible method (w2gm) with broad empirical evaluation across many standard similarity and entailment datasets and compares to strong baselines (word2vec, w2g, and multi-prototype methods). However, critical reproducibility and analysis elements are missing: hyperparameters and training details/code are not reported, ablations/sensitivity analyses (e.g., number of mixture components, initialization, runtime/memory scaling, statistical variance across runs) are not described, and diagnostic experiments isolating the mixture vs. unimodal gains are absent. The related work appears thorough (e.g., cites multi-prototype probabilistic approaches like [1] and nonparametric multi-embedding methods like [2]) and some limitations are noted (KL intractability for mixtures), but overall the experimental rigor and reproducibility detail are insufficient to fully support the claims. | B(4): The experimental suite is broad (many standard similarity and entailment datasets) and shows consistent gains versus word2vec and unimodal Gaussian baselines, which supports the central claim that mixtures capture polysemy better. However, important reproducibility details (hyperparameters, code) are not reported and there is no evidence of systematic ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., number of components, covariance choices), which would clarify when the mixture model is necessary versus simpler multi-prototype or vector methods like [1],[2]. Related work appears to be covered thoroughly and the paper candidly notes analytic limitations (KL for mixtures), but discussion of failure cases and deeper error analysis is limited. Overall the work is strong empirically and conceptually but missing some experimental controls and reproducibility detail, so I rate it a 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a plausible method (w2gm) with broad empirical evaluation across many standard similarity and entailment datasets and compares to strong baselines (word2vec, w2g, and multi-prototype methods). However, critical reproducibility and analysis elements are missing: hyperparameters and training details/code are not reported, ablations/sensitivity analyses (e.g., number of mixture components, initialization, runtime/memory scaling, statistical variance across runs) are not described, and diagnostic experiments isolating the mixture vs. unimodal gains are absent. The related work appears thorough (e.g., cites multi-prototype probabilistic approaches like [1] and nonparametric multi-embedding methods like [2]) and some limitations are noted (KL intractability for mixtures), but overall the experimental rigor and reproducibility detail are insufficient to fully support the claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental suite is broad (many standard similarity and entailment datasets) and shows consistent gains versus word2vec and unimodal Gaussian baselines, which supports the central claim that mixtures capture polysemy better. However, important reproducibility details (hyperparameters, code) are not reported and there is no evidence of systematic ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., number of components, covariance choices), which would clarify when the mixture model is necessary versus simpler multi-prototype or vector methods like [1],[2]. Related work appears to be covered thoroughly and the paper candidly notes analytic limitations (KL for mixtures), but discussion of failure cases and deeper error analysis is limited. Overall the work is strong empirically and conceptually but missing some experimental controls and reproducibility detail, so I rate it a 4.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims—that Gaussian-mixture word embeddings with an expected likelihood kernel yield an analytically tractable, trainable probabilistic multi-sense representation and improve on point and unimodal Gaussian baselines—are generally supported by experiments across many standard similarity and entailment datasets and comparisons to appropriate baselines (word2vec, w2g, and multi-prototype methods). The mathematical choice of the expected likelihood kernel to retain closed-form pairwise energies is plausible and consistent with the stated limitation that KL for mixtures lacks a closed form. However, important reproducibility and methodological details are missing in the summary: hyperparameters and training details are not reported, there is no mention of multiple runs or statistical significance testing, and few diagnostic ablations (e.g., sensitivity to number of mixture components, mixture vs. unimodal contributions) are described. These are minor but important gaps that do not overturn the core technical contribution, hence a score of 4. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported: the Gaussian-mixture representation (w2gm) paired with an expected-likelihood kernel and a max-margin ranking objective is a coherent, tractable formulation and experiments compare against sensible baselines (word2vec, unimodal Gaussians, and multi-prototype prior work) on standard similarity and entailment benchmarks. Mathematical choices are reasonable (using the ELK because KL for mixtures is intractable) and the optimization objective is standard for distributional models; I see no glaring derivation errors in the summary. Minor concerns remain about missing reported training details/hyperparameters and the reliance on ELK as the asymmetric notion of similarity (KL for mixtures is acknowledged as unavailable), which slightly weakens reproducibility and interpretation, but do not invalidate the main conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims—that Gaussian-mixture word embeddings with an expected likelihood kernel yield an analytically tractable, trainable probabilistic multi-sense representation and improve on point and unimodal Gaussian baselines—are generally supported by experiments across many standard similarity and entailment datasets and comparisons to appropriate baselines (word2vec, w2g, and multi-prototype methods). The mathematical choice of the expected likelihood kernel to retain closed-form pairwise energies is plausible and consistent with the stated limitation that KL for mixtures lacks a closed form. However, important reproducibility and methodological details are missing in the summary: hyperparameters and training details are not reported, there is no mention of multiple runs or statistical significance testing, and few diagnostic ablations (e.g., sensitivity to number of mixture components, mixture vs. unimodal contributions) are described. These are minor but important gaps that do not overturn the core technical contribution, hence a score of 4.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported: the Gaussian-mixture representation (w2gm) paired with an expected-likelihood kernel and a max-margin ranking objective is a coherent, tractable formulation and experiments compare against sensible baselines (word2vec, unimodal Gaussians, and multi-prototype prior work) on standard similarity and entailment benchmarks. Mathematical choices are reasonable (using the ELK because KL for mixtures is intractable) and the optimization objective is standard for distributional models; I see no glaring derivation errors in the summary. Minor concerns remain about missing reported training details/hyperparameters and the reliance on ELK as the asymmetric notion of similarity (KL for mixtures is acknowledged as unavailable), which slightly weakens reproducibility and interpretation, but do not invalidate the main conclusions.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against the right and recent baselines: point embeddings (word2vec sg), unimodal Gaussian embeddings (Vilnis & McCallum 2014), and multiple multi-prototype approaches (Huang et al., Tian et al., Chen et al.), and evaluates on a broad suite of standard datasets and metrics (Spearman, AP, F1, KL, etc.). Comparisons appear extensive and directly relevant, but the summary lacks details on matched training conditions, hyperparameter reporting, multiple runs/statistical tests, and whether prior methods occasionally match or outperform the proposed model — leaving some fairness/reproducibility questions. Related work coverage is noted as thorough and the most relevant competing approaches are cited, so overall the empirical comparison is strong but missing minor but important experimental-control and reporting details. | B(4): The paper compares to standard and recent baselines including word2vec, unimodal Gaussian embeddings (Vilnis & McCallum 2014), and multiple multi-prototype vector methods (Huang et al., Tian et al., Chen et al.), evaluating across many datasets and consistent metrics, which is appropriate. Comparisons appear fair in terms of datasets and metrics, and the paper analyzes key methodological differences (analytic tractability of the expected likelihood kernel, KL issues for mixtures). However, the claim of being the first probabilistic multi-meaning model seems to overlook closely related probabilistic multi-prototype work (e.g., a 2014 probabilistic multi-prototype model), so an important direct probabilistic baseline may be missing or under-discussed; it’s not clear the paper acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably. Overall, comparisons are strong but have a notable omission in the most directly relevant prior probabilistic approach.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against the right and recent baselines: point embeddings (word2vec sg), unimodal Gaussian embeddings (Vilnis & McCallum 2014), and multiple multi-prototype approaches (Huang et al., Tian et al., Chen et al.), and evaluates on a broad suite of standard datasets and metrics (Spearman, AP, F1, KL, etc.). Comparisons appear extensive and directly relevant, but the summary lacks details on matched training conditions, hyperparameter reporting, multiple runs/statistical tests, and whether prior methods occasionally match or outperform the proposed model — leaving some fairness/reproducibility questions. Related work coverage is noted as thorough and the most relevant competing approaches are cited, so overall the empirical comparison is strong but missing minor but important experimental-control and reporting details.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares to standard and recent baselines including word2vec, unimodal Gaussian embeddings (Vilnis & McCallum 2014), and multiple multi-prototype vector methods (Huang et al., Tian et al., Chen et al.), evaluating across many datasets and consistent metrics, which is appropriate. Comparisons appear fair in terms of datasets and metrics, and the paper analyzes key methodological differences (analytic tractability of the expected likelihood kernel, KL issues for mixtures). However, the claim of being the first probabilistic multi-meaning model seems to overlook closely related probabilistic multi-prototype work (e.g., a 2014 probabilistic multi-prototype model), so an important direct probabilistic baseline may be missing or under-discussed; it’s not clear the paper acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably. Overall, comparisons are strong but have a notable omission in the most directly relevant prior probabilistic approach.

main_claims: Introduce the first probabilistic word embedding that models multiple meanings per word using a Gaussian mixture model (w2gm).
Use an expected likelihood kernel energy function to retain analytic tractability and scalability when comparing distributional embeddings.
Train the Gaussian mixture embeddings with a max-margin energy-based ranking objective (skip-gram style) to capture semantic similarity and entailment.
Demonstrate superior performance of the mixture model versus point embeddings (word2vec skip-gram) and unimodal Gaussian embeddings (w2g) on standard word similarity and entailment benchmarks.
_summary.methodology: Represent each word as a Gaussian mixture and learn all mixture parameters via an energy-based max-margin ranking objective; use the expected likelihood kernel (inner product between distributions) as the energy function for closed-form similarity computation.
datasets: Baroni et al. (2012) word entailment dataset
metrics: KL divergence (used as asymmetric similarity for entailment)
limitations: Unimodal Gaussian embeddings (prior work) cannot capture multiple distinct meanings and can have overly large variances for polysemous words.
KL divergence between two Gaussian mixtures does not have a closed form (not suitable as an analytic energy for mixtures).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - probabilistic word embeddings / word representation

reviewer_a.name: Methodical Reproducibility Reviewer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer and researcher with extensive experience implementing and evaluating word embedding and representation learning systems in both research and production environments. Regularly audits experimental methodology and reproducibility for top-tier ML/NLP conferences.
traits: Values thorough experimental design: checks for strong baselines, hyperparameter sweeps, multiple runs, and statistical significance.
Insists on ablations and diagnostic experiments that isolate contributions (e.g., mixture vs. unimodal vs. point embeddings, energy function choices).
Focuses on implementation and scalability details: training time, memory, convergence behavior, and sensitivity to initialization and number of mixture components.
Looks for reproducibility artifacts: clear training procedures, exact objective, dataset splits, hyperparameters, and availability of code and pretrained models.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Pragmatic and detail-oriented: I evaluate whether the experiments credibly support the claims and whether the method can be reliably reproduced and deployed.

reviewer_b.name: Domain Impact Reviewer
reviewer_b.background: Senior NLP researcher with a long track record in distributional semantics and representation learning, who follows progress in polysemy modeling, probabilistic embeddings, and semantic evaluation benchmarks.
traits: Assesses novelty relative to prior work on multimodal/probabilistic embeddings, sense representations, and Gaussian embeddings; values clear positioning in the literature.

Values theoretical and conceptual clarity: how well the probabilistic mixture formulation addresses polysemy, interpretability of components, and connections to linguistic phenomena (e.g., senses, entailment).
Considers broader impact and applicability: whether the model enables new analyses or downstream tasks (sense induction, improved entailment models) and trade-offs versus simpler alternatives.

Attends to evaluation quality beyond benchmark scores: choice of datasets, whether metrics capture intended phenomena (polysemy, entailment), and qualitative analyses (example components, nearest neighbors per mode).
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Contextual and big-picture: I judge the work by its novelty, conceptual contribution, and potential to influence future research and applications in modeling word meaning.
